title: Task-Centric Acceleration of Small-Language Models
abstract: Small language models (SLMs) have emerged as efficient alternatives to large language mod- els for task-specific applications. However, they are often employed in high-volume, low- latency settings, where efficiency is crucial. We propose TASC, Task-Adaptive Sequence Com- pression, a framework for SLM acceleration comprising two use-cases: When performing SLM fine-tuning, we propose TASC-ft, which iteratively enriches the tokenizer vocabulary with high-frequency output n-grams and then fine-tunes the model to utilize the expanded vo- cabulary. Next, we propose an inference-time method, termed TASC-spec. TASC-spec is a lightweight, training-free speculative decoding method that constructs an n-gram draft model from the task’s output corpus, mixing task and context n-gram information. TASC-spec avoids any additional training, while avoiding draft-target vocabulary alignment constraints. We demonstrate the effectiveness of both meth- ods across multiple low output-variability gen- eration tasks. Our methods show consistent im- provements in inference efficiency while main- taining task performance.

sections.section_title:
1
Introduction
sections.text: Small language models (SLMs) are lightweight fast models that recently rose as a viable alternative to large language models (LLMs) (Belcak et al., 2025; Subramanian et al., 2025). SLMs are often distilled versions of LLMs that pose specific capabilities, with the intention to operate as a part of a multi- agent system (Lee et al., 2025; Sharma and Mehta, 2025) or in highly memory-bound high-latency set- tings, such as on-device deployment (Kementched- jhieva and Chalkidis, 2023; Kandala et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2025). SLMs, while lighter than their larger counterparts, have demonstrated compara- ble performance when fine-tuned (FT) on specific tasks, from text classification (Luo et al., 2023a; *Work was done during an internship at Amazon. Sun et al., 2023a; Sakai and Lam, 2025) and sum- marization (Xu et al., 2025; Bailly et al., 2025), to question-answering (QA) (Pappas et al., 2022; Kweon et al., 2024) and code generation (Luo et al., 2023b; Pinnaparaju et al., 2024). We argue that tasks naturally suited for SLM FT also often exhibit lower output variability com- pared to open-ended multi-purpose text generation. This manifests in a decrease of the n-gram empir- ical entropy in the dataset of expected task out- puts. That is, expected output texts tend to contain more n-grams that repeat across different samples. These n-grams often represent common phrases and terms in the task’s expected outputs. We ana- lyze this phenomenon through the lenses of typi- cality (Cover, 1999, Chapter 2). To accelerate LLMs, most prior work focus on either decreasing memory requirements or accel- erating input processing, with the purpose of han- dling longer contexts. Popular methods in these direction are model quantization (Lang et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2024), KV caching (Pope et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024a) and attention optimization (Dao et al., 2022; Kwon et al., 2023). On the contrary, in this work we follow the low output variability, com- mon in FT SLMs, and focus on the model’s outputs. Specifically, we view efficiency in terms of decreas- ing the total number of model auto-regressive steps required to generate a response from a given task input, which is a known bottleneck in autoregres- sive generation (OpenAI, 2025). To that end, our framework comprising two solutions, which we term Task-Adaptive Sequence Compression (TASC). Each solution targets a different operational SLM use-case. Figure 1 outlines TASC’s pipeline. First, when task-specific fine-tuning of the SLM is performed, we propose TASC-ft, a tokenizer augmentation procedure that explicitly leverages the task-induced concentration of output n-grams. Given a train corpus of expected task outputs, we identify high-frequency n-grams whose merge into arXiv:2602.24174v1 [cs.CL] 27 Feb 2026 Output Corpus ”The patient was diagnosed with congenital bronchopulmonary malformation (pulmonary hypoplasia) and was treated with appropriate medical management …” Collect ngrams via merge reward Init. Embeddings & Fine tune Less tokens per output mal formation bronch op Build corpus ngram model Speculative decoding with n-gram drafter monary (‘diagnosed’ , ‘with’) (‘ a’, 0.59) (‘diagnosed’ , ‘with’) (‘ acute’, 0.177) (‘diagnosed’ , ‘with’) (‘ an’, 0.161) (‘diagnosed’ , ‘with’) (‘ primary’, 0.07) n-gram drafter n-gram drafter n-gram drafter Target LLM The patient was patient disease disease … ✅ ✅ ❌ n-gram prediction ”The patient was diagnosed with congenital bronchopulmonary malformation (pulmonary hypoplasia) and was treated with appropriate medical management …” (24 tokens) (15 tokens) The … Figure 1: Proposed method for a given task corpus. TASC-ft (upper) augments the model’s tokenizer with n-gram tokens and trains their embeddings during the model’s fine-tune. TASC-spec (lower) accelerates the model in inference-time using an n-gram model drafter, providing significant speedup with no additional training. single tokens yields the largest reduction in ex- pected output length across the corpus. We itera- tively enrich the model’s tokenizer with such task- specific tokens using a byte pair encoding (BPE) inspired scheme, and fine-tune the SLM to properly utilize the expanded vocabulary. This procedure reduces the number of decoding steps required to generate typical task outputs, resulting in substan- tial latency improvements while preserving task performance. Second, when model fine-tuning is not per- formed (e.g., when using off-the-shelf fine-tuned SLMs for rapid development) we propose a training-free inference-time acceleration method. Inspired by speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023), we construct a lightweight n-gram draft model directly from the task’s output corpus. The n-gram drafter generates sequence predictions at a negligible cost, which are then verified by the target model in a single forward pass. To balance global task structure with instance-specific informa- tion, we combine global corpus-derived n-grams with local prompt-derived information into a uni- fied draft model, which we term TASC-spec. This approach yields meaningful speedups without ad- ditional training. Furthermore, while most existing methods require both the target and draft to share the same vocabulary – limiting the cross-model applicability of speculative decoding – TASC-spec automatically adapts to the target’s vocabulary. Our main contributions are as follows:

sections.section_title:
1. We characterize the set of generation tasks by
the relation between their input vs. output
variability, which we then formalize through
the information-theoretic perspective of typi-

2. We propose TASC-ft, an algorithm that en-
riches the SLM’s tokenizer vocabulary with
task-specific n-grams.
TASC-ft integrates
seamlessly into parameter-efficient FT, achiev-
ing up to ×2.1 reductions in model runtime
while preserving generation quality.

3. We show that the token distribution’s 2-Rényi
entropy can act as a predictive measure of fine-
tuned model acceleration under TASC-ft.

4. We introduce TASC-spec, a lightweight,
training-free speculative decoding method
that uses n-gram draft models. TASC-spec
exhibits inference speedups of up to ×3.15
across multiple generation tasks.

2
Related Work
sections.text: Tokenizer augmentation Popularized by (Hong et al., 2021; Sachidananda et al., 2021), modifica- tion of the tokenizer’s vocabulary had been recently adopted as a method for improving the model’s domain-adaptation abilities and sequence compres- sion capabilities. (Gee et al., 2023) shows that adding the top-K most frequent n-grams improved input sequence compression for BERT-like models and (Schmidt et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2025) goes be- yond pre-tokenization boundaries to include bigger token merges. The most relevant work is Adap- tiVocab (Nakash et al., 2025), which proposes a domain-based vocabulary adaptation method. Do- main adaptation targets the input corpus, thus re- quiring a significant modification of the tokenizer vocabulary. In contrast, our setting is task-driven: Bigram Entropy # Unique Bigrams For 80% CDF Task Input Output ∆H Input Output Ratio Sentiment Analysis (Finance) 14.5 2.21 ↓−84.7% 33.53K 4 8383 Yes/No Questions (Medical) 15.34 3 ↓−80.48% 17.01K 3 5698 POS Tagging 15.53 6.57 ↓−57.7% 65.3K 63 1036.3 Intent Classification & Slot Fill 13.4 9.74 ↓−37.45% 15.943K 774 20.6 Massive Classification (Legal) 14.43 12.76 ↓−11.6% 36.8K 8.34K 4.41 Abbreviation Expansion (Medical) 16.17 14.19 ↓−12.24% 195.1K 84.28K 2.32 Summarization (Medical) 16.14 15.39 ↓−4.86% 17.14K 15.16K 1.13 Summarization (News) 17.71 15.79 ↓−10.8% 387.7K 59.34K 6.52 Summarization (Legal) 16.8 15.8 ↓−5.9% 229.9K 89.14K 2.73 Question Answering (Medical) 16.2 15.38 ↓−5.04% 196.34K 111.6K 1.76 Creative Writing 12.75 16.62 ↑+30.4% 11.16K 168.81K 0.06 Table 1: Word bigram distribution analysis across several generation tasks. We compare the Shannon entropy and the number of unique word bigrams that cover 80% of the bigram empirical distribution on an empirical dataset. The specific dataset names for each task is given in Appendix B. while inputs are linguistically diverse, the task in- duces low output variability. This asymmetry leads to different design choices. Our method adds a small number of task-specific tokens, capturing most of the relevant variability without sacrificing generality or requiring token removal, and explic- itly targeting inference efficiency rather than broad domain coverage. Speculative Decoding: Popularized by (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023), spec- ulative decoding is nowadays a common method for acceleration of LLMs using a fast auxiliary draft model. The authors of (Fu et al., 2024) study draft parallel decoding using Jacobi iterations. In (Cai et al., 2024), a multi-head drafter is proposed, where each head learns to predict a different lookahead step. The authors of (Li et al., 2024b) transform the speculative decoding problem to the feature space via EAGLE. (Sun et al., 2023b) shows that speculative decoding is theoretically optimal using optimal transport theory. Most related work in this field is n-grammys (Stewart et al., 2024), in which a simple bigram model that assigns each token its most probable next token under the given model. However, n-grammys is not task-specific, trading generality with performance. n-Grams for LLMs: Despite the dominance of transformer-based models, n-gram statistics remain relevant for efficiency, adaptation, and analysis. Early works combined neural models with external n-gram LMs to inject domain-specific biases with- out retraining (Gulcehre et al., 2015), while later retrieval-based methods generalized n-gram mem- orization through nearest-neighbor interpolation (Khandelwal et al., 2019). In a related direction, Infini-grams (Liu et al., 2024) leverage unbounded- length n-gram statistics to analyze and approximate LLM next-token behavior. In (Diao et al., 2021) n-gram information is incorporated as explicit fea- tures or auxiliary representations. Recent analyses further suggest that LLMs often behave similarly to high-order n-gram models over substantial por- tions of the token distribution (Nguyen, 2024; Varre et al., 2025).

sections.section_title:
3
Task-Specific Output Structure and
Efficiency
sections.text: While SLMs are capable of open-ended generation, many practical applications rely on constrained generation tasks. In these scenarios, the output is not free-form but strictly bound by a predefined la- bel set, structural schema, or other specific require- ments. In this section, we formalize and quantify this observation through an information-theoretic perspective.

sections.section_title:
3.1
Task-Induced n-Grams Concentration
sections.text: Consider some generation task τ that has a repre- sentative corpus, i.e., a collection of input texts and corresponding expected outputs. We denote the set of expected outputs for a given task τ by Eτ. For some finite n, we consider the empirical output n- gram distribution based on the n-gram frequencies in Eτ. 1 This is a conditional distribution, where the conditioning is on τ. For example, in POS-tagging, expected output are sequences of discrete part-of- speech labels (e.g., DET, NOUN, VERB), drawn from a small, fixed tag set. Here, the n-gram distribu- tion is significantly skewed towards specific label 1As we are yet to consider a specific tokenizer, we can, without loss of generality, consider word n-grams. sequences, unlike the heavier-tailed distribution of open-ended text. Formally, the skewed n-gram distribution ex- hibits reduced entropy: H(T n|T = τ) ≪H(T n), (1) where T n ≜(T1, . . . Tn) is the n-gram random variable and H(T n) is its entropy, unconditioned on any specific τ.2 Following the information the- oretic notion of typicality (Shannon, 1948; Cover, 1999), the size of the set containing most probable task output n-grams, denoted An,τ, is determined by this conditional entropy: An,τ ≈exp (H(T n|T = τ)) . (2) We call An,t the typical set of output n-grams given generation task τ. An,t is potentially ex- ponentially smaller than the full space of n-grams. Consequently, a relatively small number of high- probability n-grams dominate the task-conditioned distribution. We demonstrate this phenomenon across a range of tasks, using word bigrams for simplicity. For each dataset, we measure the bigram entropy and the number of unique bigrams that attain 80% of the bigram CDF. Both metrics serve as proxies for the size and dominance of the typical set of bigrams in Eτ. As shown in Table 1, some generation tasks result in a small set of bigrams that cover a large portion of the bigram distribution. We examine output variability in a comparative manner against the inputs, assuming inputs are provided in free- form natural language.3 Notably, the tasks at hand exist along a spectrum, defined in terms of the input vs. output variabil- ity. To capture this, we measure the difference in entropy and CDF coverage ratio between the input and output bigram distributions. In highly constrained tasks, such as sentiment analysis, the typical set is very small, implying that most of the output distribution is concentrated on a small set. Conversely, in tasks such as creative writing, outputs exhibit higher variability than inputs. The comparative quantification distinguishes tasks from domains; while fixing a domain may reduce variability, this reduction applies to both inputs and outputs, and not necessarily in the same 2Formally, we can take a uniform prior over possible tasks and define H(T n) ≜|T |−1 P τ∈T H(T n|T = τ). 3We make this assumption as often those may contain human-written texts. Figure 2: Effect of added tokens on the tokenized output texts in the EUR-LEX dataset, nmax = 4. magnitude as outputs may have their unique em- phasis or style. In contrast, as shown in Table 1, fixing a task primarily affects the output distribu- tion. Consequently, the dominant factor influencing the difference in input vs. output variability is the type of task, rather than the domain. For exam- ple, Yes/No QA exhibits a significant reduction in output variability compared to summarization, al- though both tasks operate within the medical texts domain. In what follows, we propose TASC, which aims to accelerate SLMs whose target is low output variability tasks (except for simple classification tasks that have extremely low variability and don’t require acceleration). As we later explain, each task is aimed at a different use-case and aims to address a different regime of the variability spectrum.

sections.section_title:
4
Tokenizer Augmentation Method
sections.text: When fine-tuning on the task at hand is both de- sired and feasible, we extend the standard fine- tuning procedure with a tokenizer preprocessing step. Specifically, we define a score for merging an n-gram of tokens into a single new token. The score is used in a BPE-inspired algorithm to itera- tively expand the tokenizer vocabulary with high- scoring n-grams. The new n-gram token embed- dings are optimized alongside the fine tuning pro- cedure.

sections.section_title:
4.1
Enriching the Tokenizer
sections.text: Let Vbase denote the model’s vocabulary and let Eτ be the outputs of the train set. We treat it as a rep- resentative corpus of the task’s outputs distribution, consisting of token sequences over Vbase. Notably, Eτ contains only expected outputs, as our goal is to reduce decoding-time latency rather than pre- fill cost. We propose a BPE-inspired scheme4 that 4See Appendix A for information on BPE. iteratively adds token n-grams to Vbase with the purpose of reducing the expected output length in Eτ. To identify potential token n-gram candidates, we define the merge reward of each token n-gram tn by its expected contribution to sequence-length reduction: fmerge(tn) ≜freq(tn) · (n −1), (3) where freq(tn) is the n-gram’s frequency in Eτ and the factor (n −1) corresponds to the net reduc- tion in token count, if we were to merge a single occurrence of tn into a single token. While the merge reward captures expected sequence-length reduction, introducing a new n- gram token can bias greedy decoding by collapsing multiple plausible continuations into a single dom- inant path, suppressing alternatives whose prefix collides with the new token’s suffix. For instance, adding the bigram (’in’, ’the’) as a new token may lead the model to favor this continuation over in- dividually less frequent but collectively probable alternatives like ’therapy’ or ’theorem’, which share the prefix ’the’. To mitigate this effect, we propose the Prefix Collision Score (PCS), which measures the probability that the suffix of an n- gram collides with prefixes of existing tokens. For- mally, for an n-gram tn, let str(tn) be its string representation. We define: PCS(tn) = P t′∈V str(tn) is a prefix of (str(t′) for t′ ∈Vi over Eτ. We therefore add tn to Vi only if PCS(tn) falls below a prescribed threshold. Starting from V0 = Vbase, at each step i we ex- tend Vi with the element that maximizes fmerge(tn) and that does not pass the PCS threshold. This repeats until a predefined vocabulary budget is reached or a predetermined number of iterations are performed. We refer to this method as TASC-ft and outline its steps in Algorithm 1. Figure 2 demon- strates the effect of TASC-ft on EUR-LEX, a mas- sive multi-label classification dataset (Chalkidis et al., 2019), in which every text has up to 26 labels out of 4K classes. We evaluate the average output length and measure the output compression rate over the tokenized validation set. Consequently, with only 1000 new tokens we obtain ×2.65 re- duction in the average output length and ×2.67 increase in the bytes-per token metric. We find additional motivation in the uniform information distribution (UID) hypothesis (Jaeger Algorithm 1 TASC-ft: Tokenizer Enrichment Require: Eτ, Vbase, token budget M, nmax, PCS threshold αPCS 1: Initialize i = 0, usedNgrams = set() 2: Set Vi = Vbase 3: while i < M do 4: Ngrams = collectNgrams(Vi, nmax) 5: Calculate fmerge for tn ∈Ngrams 6: Take t⋆∈arg maxtn∈Vn i fmerge(tn) 7: if PCS(t⋆) < αPCS then 8: Vi ←Vi ∪(t⋆) 9: i ←i + 1 10: end if 11: usedNgrams ←t⋆ 12: end while 13: return Enriched tokenizer vocabulary V and Levy, 2006), which posits that efficient linguis- tic representations tend to distribute information evenly across tokens, leading to approximately uni- form per-token surprisal along sequences. This principle motivates our procedure. By collapsing typical high-probability n-grams into single tokens, the induced token distribution over the enriched vocabulary becomes less peaked, increasing the to- ken distribution entropy and expanding the typical set. For example, on the EUR-LEX dataset, enrich- ing the tokenizer with 1000 task-specific n-grams increases the normalized entropy of the empirical output token distribution from 0.423 to 0.592 (see Appendix D for more information).

sections.section_title:
4.2
Integration with Model Fine-Tuning
sections.text: Equipped with the extended tokenizer, we inte- grate the new tokens into the model’s architecture. This requires resizing the model’s input embed- ding matrix and the LM head to accommodate the new n-gram tokens. For a new n-gram token tnew = (t1, . . . , tn), we initialize its embedding vector as the mean of its constituent tokens em- beddings enew = 1 n Pn j=1 etj. This initialization provides a strong semantic prior, ensuring stable training with minimal adaptation overhead. We apply this initialization strategy to both the input embeddings and the LM head weights. During the task-specific fine-tuning phase, these new parameters are optimized jointly with the model’s low-rank adapters. Importantly, the ad- ditional training overhead remains modest and is compatible with single-GPU fine-tuning regimes commonly associated with SLM deployment. For example, when fine-tuning a Qwen2.5-3B model with r = 16, adding 500 new tokens results in an increase of the total trainable parameters by 6%. As shown in Section 6, this integration yields sig- nificant efficiency gains in inference latency while maintaining output generation quality. 5 n-Gram Speculative Decoding Method When fine tuning is not intended or a fine-tuned model is already available, we can still use the ideas from Section 3 to accelerate the model under the given task. We follow a speculative decoding proce- dure building upon the typical set of n-grams in Eτ. The resulting method is a training-free inference- time acceleration algorithm that is model agnostic.

sections.section_title:
5.1
Speculative Decoding
sections.text: Speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023) is a recent inference acceleration methodology for LLMs in memory-bound environments, which fol- lows a ‘draft, then verify’ scheme. In speculative decoding, a fast draft model generates a sequence of γ tokens, which are then verified by the slower target model in parallel via a single forward pass. Following verification, a subsequence of k ≤γ tokens is accepted. The key trade-off in speculative decoding lies between the target’s acceptance rate of draft tokens and the draft generation time. Con- ceptually, faster drafts typically approximate the target distribution less accurately, while increas- ing acceptance rates often comes at the price of slowing the draft model.

sections.section_title:
5.2
Proposed Method: TASC-spec
sections.text: Our goal is to construct an n-gram draft model that captures the typical n-gram set in Eτ. First, fix nmax ∈N. The drafter is a collection of n-gram models Mn for n ≤nmax. Each Mn is derived from the n-gram frequencies in Eτ. To retain only sig- nificant n-grams, we prune each Mn by removing elements whose frequency is below a prescribed threshold pmin. Consequently, we define the corpus n-gram drafter as pcorp ≜S n≤nmax Mn. Given a prefix c = tnmax we obtain pcorp(·|c) by querying (Mn)n≤nmax in descending order of n. We return the first matching prediction for c in pcorp, or the most frequent token in V if none was found. Since pcorp is derived solely from Eτ, it mostly captures global task information, while context- specific information may be valuable for the next token prediction. For example, in QA, overloading the previous context term captures specific phrases that are useful for the generation, but are not nec- essarily in Eτ. We incorporate this local, context- dependent information by combining the corpus n- gram with a prompt-based n-ngram drafter pprompt. The prompt drafter is obtained similarly to pcorp, but it is based on the available context. The result- ing n-gram drafter, termed TASC-spec, is a mixture distribution. For some λ ∈[0, 1] it is given by pmixed(x|c) = λpcorp(x|c) + (1 −λ)pprompt(x|c). TASC-spec synergies global and local information. The proposed method alleviates several impor- tant bottlenecks of existing speculative decoding methods. First, it does not require an additional parametric drafter model, thus reducing memory, the main overhead of speculative decoding. Fur- thermore, the method is training-free, i.e., fine- tuning the target model does not require re-training the drafter. Next, the method only requires that Eτ faithfully represent the target’s output distribution – a reasonable assumption when Eτ is derived from the target’s training data. Finally, it is tokenizer- agnostic as TASC-ft operates on the token-level. It is therefore independent of any specific tokenizer choices and tokenizer-matching, which is a current issue in the speculative decoding literature with only preliminary solutions (Timor et al., 2025). Trade-off between methods. As noted in Sec- tion 3, tasks can be arranged according to output n-grams variability, which is a proxy for typical n-gram set’s size. When the typical set is small, TASC-ft with a reasonable token budget has a higher probability of covering a large portion of it, implying higher potential efficiency gains. How- ever, when the set is large, TASC-ft would require a larger budget to succeed, resulting in a complex fine-tune procedure. In such cases, TASC-spec would be favorable, as it remains a lightweight so- lution compared to an LLM draft alternative even if it considers a large amount of n-grams.

sections.section_title:
6
Results
sections.text: We study the empirical performance of the pro- posed methods on a set of experiments spanning 4 datasets. All experiments were performed on a single Tesla V-100 GPU (32GB). Hyperparameter values are based on preliminary evaluations and ablations. Full details are given in Appendix B. # Added Tokens 0 150 300 500 750 1000 Judge Score 3.53 ± 0.73 3.21 ± 0.79 3.15 ± 0.82 3.23 ± 0.77 3.25 ± 0.76 3.18 ± 0.79 Table 2: LLM judge results vs. Number of added tokens, TASC-ft, Asclepius dataset. (a) EUR-LEX (massive classification) (b) Asclepius (medical QA) (c) MASSIVE (ICSF) Figure 3: TASC-ft: Task performance metric vs. average runtime vs. Number of Added tokens. Each dataset has a different metric according to its task: F1 for EUR-LEX, Bert-score for Asclepius and join accurcay for MASSIVE.

sections.section_title:
6.1
TASC-ft: Tokenizer Augmentation
sections.text: We test the effect of TASC-ft on the resulting fine- tuned model. We measure the effect of the added tokens (and consequently, trainable parameters) on the trained model’s performance. Our baseline is the same base model, going through similar train- ing, but without the newly introduced n-gram to- kens. In all experiments we consider a Qwen2.5- 0.5B base model, employ QLoRa optimization (Dettmers et al., 2023) and train for 3 epochs. Due to space constraints, additional performance met- rics and ablations are reported in Appendix C. Datasets. We consider three tasks: (i) massive text classification for which we use the EUR-LEX dataset (Chalkidis et al., 2019). It contains 19.6K policy texts, each labeled with up to 26 labels out of a total of 4K possible labels, (ii) Medical QA, for which we consider the Asclepius dataset (Kweon et al., 2024). We focus on the QA category that consists of 11K QA pairs, (iii) Intent classification & slot filling (ICSF), for which we consider the MASSIVE dataset (FitzGerald et al., 2023) that contains of 16.5K english utterances with intent and slot fills labels. The dataset contains 50 distinct intent categories and 60 slot fill categories. Metrics. For each dataset, we measure the aver- age time to generate a response and calculate the response’s quality on a task-specific evaluation met- ric. For EUR-LEX we consider the F1 score, and for Asclepius we consider both the BERT score (Zhang et al., 2019) and a LLM-judge score, based on the evaluation prompt from (Kweon et al., 2024) using Claude4.5-Sonnet as the judge. For ICSF we consider both intent accuracy, slot fill F1 score and joint accuracy. To follow a real-world use case, we consider batched generation with b = 8. Results. We examine the effect of the number of tokens added, comparing to the same base model undergoing comparable training without newly in- troduced n-gram tokens. As shown in Figure 3, training with the extended tokenizer preserves task performance while achieving faster average gen- eration time on the test set. Table 2 measures a judge-LLM evaluations of the Asclepius outputs, showing negligible impact on the output quality. Our experiments consider a token budget of up to 1000 n-grams. While increasing the number of added tokens consistently reduces inference-time generation latency, the marginal gains diminish as the budget grows, indicating a trade-off between acceleration and the training parameters overhead. In practice, the effective cutoff improvement in our experiments is no larger than 500 tokens, which is significantly smaller than the token budgets typi- cally used in domain-base tokenization adaptations (Nakash et al., 2025). We leave a principled analy- sis of the optimal TASC-ft budget for future work. Predicting Runtime Reduction. Fine-tuning is computationally expensive, motivating the need for a lightweight proxy to assess a model’s poten- tial benefit from TASC-ft. We examine whether a dataset-level statistic can serve as a reliable predic- tor of inference runtime. Specifically, we consider the 2-Rényi entropy (H2) of the token distribution, previously linked to downstream performance. Our Figure 4: Acceptance rate vs. speculated token position. experiments comprise 124 runs across 8 configura- tions, where runs within each configuration differ only in the token budget. We consider two com- plementary criteria: (i) Kendall’s τ, computed sep- arately for each configuration, yields τ < −0.9 (p < 0.005) in all cases, indicating a strong and consistent inverse correlation. We measure the rank correlation using Kendall’s τ, all yielding τ < −0.9 with p-value < 0.005. This indicates a near-perfect inverse agreement. (ii) Following (Pe- saran and Timmermann, 1992), we asses how often a change in H2 correctly predicts the direction of change in runtime. We observed a 92% success rate, confirming that H2 is a highly reliable indi- cator for predicting runtime reduction. Additional details are provided in Appendix E.

sections.section_title:
6.2
TASC-spec: Inference-Time Acceleration
sections.text: We compare our results with the n-grammy’s method (Stewart et al., 2024), which proposes a bigram-based drafter that is obtained by taking the conditional LLM distribution for each element in the tokenizer’s vocabulary. To maintain compara- bility we consider task fine-tuned LLMs, to ensure that its conditional distribution is adapted to the task as hand. We consider a beam-size of b = 1 and apply TASC-spec with pmin = 5 and λ = 0.75. Datasets and Models We consider two medi- cal QA tasks, given by the Asclepius (Kweon et al., 2024) and MedQuaD (Ben Abacha and Demner-Fushman, 2019) datasets, and EUR-LEX. Asclepius focuses on reading comprehension of medical texts, whereas MedQuaD does not contain an additional context for each question. Speedup over target model. We evaluate perfor- mance by calculating the average speedup over the target model across 50 test samples per genera- tion task. We report the performance of the corpus Dataset Method Speedup Asclepius pcorpus ×1.63 TASC-spec (ours) ×3.15 N-Grammys (bigram) ×1.03 N-Grammys (mixed) ×3.08 MedQuaD pcorpus ×1.52 TASC-spec (ours) ×1.78 N-Grammys (bigram) ×1.01 N-Grammys (mixed) ×1.32 EUR-LEX pcorpus ×1.49 TASC-spec (ours) ×2.17 N-Grammys (bigram) ×1.04 N-Grammys (mixed) ×1.98 Table 3: Target speedup results in several datasets. TASC-spec is employed with λ = 0.75. n-gram pcorp, the n-grammy’s drafter, and their mixing with the context-based n-gram drafter. As seen in Table 3, the corpus n-gram achieves a better target speedup over the n-grammy’s drafter. This advantage is maintained after mixing with pprompt, emphasizing the power of global corpus informa- tion for speculative decoding. Position-wise Acceptance. Due to the negligible cost of obtaining a prediction from n-gram mod- els we take long prediction horizons, which are then verified in-parallel by the target model, with- out suffering a significant latency overhead. We provide a finer granularity for acceptance by an- alyzing the acceptance rate for each position in TASC-spec’s continuations separately. As seen in Figure 4, the acceptance rate drops as the posi- tion index increases. This is due to the expected distribution shift of the n-gram model. Although the n-gram model is a highly simplified language model, we obtain relatively high acceptance rates in initial positions, with first position acceptance rate α = 0.57 in Asclepius and α = 0.39 in MedQuaD.

sections.section_title:
7
Conclusion
sections.text: This paper presented TASC, an SLM acceleration framework that exploits the n-gram typical set in low output variability tasks. The proposed meth- ods consider either fine-tune setting via tokenizer augmentation or inference-time acceleration using n-gram draft models. Moreover, we study the power of the token distribution’s Rényi entropy as a predictive measure of potential runtime ac- celeration. The proposed methods substantially reduce the number of decoding steps while preserv- ing output quality. For future work we consider a comprehensive analysis of the optimal token bud- get for TASC-ft following the diminishing returns in terms of newly added tokens.

sections.section_title:
8
Limitations
sections.text: The proposed methodologies suffer from several limitations. First, we assume that the considered task has an available representative dataset, which we can use for typicality analysis. We implicitly assume that the training dataset we utilize captures the underlying distribution in the test set. The suc- cess of our method is limited to generation tasks that exhibit the low output variability demonstrated in Table 1. The tokenizer augmentation procedure hinges on the assumption that we have access to the model weights and that we have the computational capa- bilities to fine-tune that model. Furthermore, by augmenting the model’s tokenizer, we may harm its generalist capabilities and decrease its performance in other tasks. However, we anticipate this effect to be less significant than both adding and remov- ing tokens from the vocabulary, as often done in domain-based methods (as, e.g., in (Nakash et al., 2025)).

In Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers), pages 5184–5207. A Additional Information on Byte-Pair Encoding BPE (Gage, 1994) is a simple frequency-based al- gorithm that constructs a subword vocabulary in a data-driven manner. Given a training corpus C, BPE learns both a vocabulary V and an ordered list of merge operations. The algorithm starts from a base vocabulary V0, typically consisting of individual characters or bytes. Each element of the corpus is initially repre- sented as a sequence of symbols from V0. At each iteration, BPE identifies the most frequent token bigram in the corpus and merges it into a new to- ken, which is added to the vocabulary. This merge operation replaces all occurrences of the selected pair with the newly created single token. The merging procedure iteratively repeats for M iterations, resulting in a final vocabulary V = V0 ∪{t⋆ 1, . . . , t⋆ M}, along with an ordered merge table that records the sequence in which merges were applied. At inference time, new text is tok- enized by greedily applying these learned merges in order, producing a sequence of tokens drawn from V. Following the HuggingFace implementa- tion of tokenizer vocabulary augmentation, when adding new tokens to an existing vocabulary, their corresponding merge operations are not added to the merge table. They are stored in a different data structure. Upon encoding, the set of new tokens is queried for a possible continuation prior to the base tokenizer’s merge table. B Additional Implementation Details B.1 LLM Judge Information The judge we use to generate the results in Table 2 is Claude-4.5 Sonnet. We deploy the LLM evalua- tion pipeline through Amazon Bedrock. The scores are calculated out of 1260 output samples. The evaluation prompt is given Figure 9. It is based the judge evaluation prompt from (Kweon et al., 2024). To avoid position bias in the response posi- tion in the evaluation prompt, we randomly select the order of LLM response in each sample. B.2 Additional Training Information Applying TASC-ft requires fine-tuning the model at hand with the updated token vocabulary. We fine-tuned the model using Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) (Dettmers et al., 2023) with 4-bit quantization via the BitsAndBytes library, employing the NF4 quantization type with double quantization enabled and bfloat16 precision for compute. We consider LoRa rank r = 16 and α = 16 (i.e., a scaling factor of 1.0), and dropout rate of 0.2. The LoRA adapters were applied to the attention layers as well as the feed-forward layers. Additionally, the embedding layer and language modeling head were retrained to accommodate the extended vocabulary with new task-specific tokens. Training was conducted with an initial learning rate of 2 × 10−4 using a linear learning rate scheduler and the AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2017). We used a per-device batch size of 8 and an effective batch size of 64. B.3 Additional Datasets and Models Information The datasets considered in Table 1 are described in Table 4. In our experiments, we consider the following datasets:

classification & slot filling dataset, curated
over 52 languages. Each sample contains an

sections.text: C Additional Experiment Results and Ablations C.1 TASC-ft: Additional Evaluation Metrics Additional Metrics in MASSIVE Dataset: We complement TASC-ft’s results on the ICSF task from Section 6 with additional metrics. Specif- ically, we measure the intent accuracy and slot prediction F1 score, to present a finer granular- ity of the fine-tuned model’s behavior on the test set. For completeness, we present these metrics alongside the metrics reported in Section 6. As seen from Table 5, both intent accuracy and slot F1 scores present a slight improvement when introduc- ing new n-gram tokens. This trend is in line with the joint accuracy, which is a coarser and stricter measure of performance. Precision-Recall: We present the fine-tuned model’s precision-recall trade off. As the main pa- rameter of our framework is the number of added tokens, we compare the trade-of on models trained with different numbers of added tokens. We recall that the latency improvement was already estab- lished and studied in Section 6. Figure 5 presents the precision-recall trade-off for two values of num- ber of training epochs. As seen from Figure 5, introducing new tokens improves precision-recall trade-off, with the best performance at M = 250 new tokens. These results are aligned with Table 5, which shows that task performance improvement after introducing new tokens, both in terms of in- tent and slot fill prediction. To that end, while existing literature often discusses a hard trade-off between runtime acceleration and output quality, we observe that this is not necessarily the case in low output variability generation with SLMs. Next, we consider the EUR-LEX dataset. We observe the precision-recall trade-off for several values of maxN, the maximal merge size in the TASC-ft algorithm (Algorithm 1). As seen in Fig- ure 6, the number of added tokens has a relatively negligible on the trade-off. However, we note a uni- form increase in precision across all experiments. C.2 Comparing TASC-ft and TASC-spec This section presents a comparison the effect of TASC-ft and TASC-spec in the EUR-LEX massive classification dataset (Chalkidis et al., 2019). The baseline is a fine-tuned model without the base tokenizer. As seen in Table 6, TASC-ft attains a better speedup of the baseline. However, it requires access to the fine-tune procedure, while TASC-spec (a) 3 Training Epochs (b) 3 Training Epochs Figure 5: Precision-recall trade off for the ICSF task. Each marker denotes a different number of newly added token n-grams to the tokenizer vocabulary prior to fine-tuning. (a) maxN = 2 (b) maxN = 3 (c) maxN = 4 Figure 6: Precision-recall trade off for the EUR-LEX task. Each marker denotes a different number of newly added token n-grams to the tokenizer vocabulary prior to fine-tuning. Number of tokens Intent accuracy (↑) Slot F1 (↑) Joint accuracy (↑) Average sample runtime [ms] (↓) 0 90.4 76.45 63.0 302 250 91.5 77.0 64.6 260 500 91.3 77.0 64.3 258.9 1000 91.1 76.2 63.4 256 Table 5: TASC-ft results on the intent classification and slot fill MASSIVE (FitzGerald et al., 2023) dataset. Method TASC-ft TASC-spec Speedup ×2.1 ×2.17 Table 6: Comparison of TASC-ft and TASC-spec. is independent of such assumption. C.3 TASC-ft: Ablation on Number of Training Epochs We experimented with running the fine-tune proce- dure for different values of training epochs. It can be seen from Table 7 that a single training epoch results in insufficient performance, while training for too many epochs results in performance sat- uration, and even decrease in some cases (which may follow from overfit). To that end, we chose to conduct experiments with 3 training epochs, which demonstrated satisfactory performance, while pro- viding us with a relatively lightweight fine-tune procedure. C.4 TASC-ft: Results on Additional Models We present additional results on the TASC-ft method by varying the utilized model. We con- sider the Mistralv02-7B model and apply it to the massive classification task. As seen from table 8, the F1 score is maintained after introducing new tokens to the LLM’s tokenizer while exhibiting a decrease in the average runtime on the test data. However, we note that the obtained efficiency gains are slightly smaller than the ones obtained for the EUR-LEX # Train Epochs F1 Avg. runtime 1 0.624 0.174 3 0.647 0.174 5 0.655 0.173 10 0.637 0.181 MASSIVE # Train Epochs Joint acc. Avg. runtime 1 0.576 0.254 3 0.634 0.256 5 0.625 0.257 Table 7: Number of training Epochs ablation, number of added tokens is M = 1000 across all runs. # Added Tokens F1 score Average Runtime [ms] 0 69.1 ± 8 1280 ± 70.1 150 68.9 ± 0.09 1220 ± 81.3 500 68.3 ± 0.5 1190 ± 79.4 1000 68.8 ± 0.5 1130 ± 90.1 Table 8: TASC-ft Results on EUR-LEX dataset with model MistralV02-7B. Qwen2.5-0.5B model. Specifically, Mistral ob- tains a speedup of ×1.19, while Qwen obtained a speedup of ×2.31. C.5 TASC-spec: Ablation on Mixing Parameter Value We test the effect of the mixing parameter λ value in TASC-spec. To do that, we curate a dedicated validation set of 150 samples from each dataset. As seen in Figure 7, For λ ∈[0.5, 0.9], we result in a mixture distribution that improves upon both the corpus n-gram and the prompt information. We note that highest speedup is attained within the interval λ ∈[0.5, 0.75], which implies that the un- derlying distributions are relatively spiky. That is, each distribution proposes a next token with rela- tively high probability in distinct cases, resulting in more accepted token overall. We note that unlike n- grammys (Stewart et al., 2024), the n-gram distribu- tions are independent of the underlying model, and that potential misalignment with the model might decrease performance, while the n-gram model properly predict the "task ground truth" next token. D Uniform Information Density - Analysis We provide empirical evidence that the effects in- duced by TASC-ft are consistent with the uniform information distribution (UID) hypothesis. UID predicts that efficient representations distribute in- formation more evenly across tokens, reducing con- centration of probability mass and stabilizing per- token surprisal along sequences. By collapsing frequent task-specific n-grams into atomic sym- bols, TASC-ft increases the size of the token larger typical set. As a result, the empirical output token distribution becomes less peaked and exhibits a heavier tail. This effect is visualized in Figure 8, where applying TASC-ft leads to a noticeably flat- ter distribution compared to the baseline tokenizer. We further quantify this uniformization by mea- suring the entropy of the output token distribution before and after applying TASC-ft. We report normalized entropy, obtained by dividing the em- pirical entropy by the maximal possible entropy, log |V|. As shown in Table 9, TASC-ft consistently increases token entropy across settings, indicating reduced redundancy. E Predicting Model Speedup in TASC-ft from the Data Our goal is to validate whether a cheap preprocessing-time statistic such as the Rényi en- tropy of order 2 (H2) can serve as a reliable proxy for predicting runtime reduction after fine-tuning. Following (Zouhar et al., 2023), Rényi entropy of the token distribution was shown to be correlated with model performance. Furthermore, we con- sider entropic measures are a proxy for the size of the typical set due to (Cover, 1999) (See Section 3). For a distribution p(x) defined on some finite set X, The Rényi entropy of order α ̸= 1 is defined as Hα(p) = 1 1 −α log x∈X p(x)α ! where limα→1 Hα(p) converges to Shannon’s en- tropy. Setting: We consider a total of 124 simula- tions, spanning over two datasets (EUR-LEX and Asclepius), two SLM sizes (Qwen2.5-0.5B and Qwen2.5-3B), 3 values of nmax (2, 3, 4) and sev- eral seed values. Each simluation is obtained as follows: We set a token budget M, apply Algo- rithm 1 to collect M tokens, measure the aug- mented token vocabulary Rényi entropy (over the (a) MedQuaD dataset (b) Asclepius dataset. Figure 7: Speedup vs. mixing parameter γ. (a) EUR-LEX dataset (massive multi-class multi-label calssification). (b) MASSIVE dataset (intent classification & slot fill). (c) Asclepius dataset (medical QA). Figure 8: Token distribution comparison before and after augmenting the tokenizer vocabulary via TASC-ft (Algorithm 1). I cane be seen that applying TASC-ft results in a more uniform token distribution. - Output Input Dataset Entropy (before) Entropy (after) Entropy (before) Entropy (after) EUR-LEX 0.423 0.592 0.4502 0.455 Asclepius 0.402 0.537 0.479 0.562 Table 9: Effect of TASC-ft on the token entropy in the output dataset. train set), fine-tune the model with the augmented tokenizer and record the average runtime on the test set. We group the samples as sequences of (M, H2(M), runtime(M)) for each set of hyper- parameter values. Our goal is to analyze the cor- relation of predictive power of H2 for fine-tuned model runtime reduction. E.1 Ordering Consistency via Rank Correlation To assess the predictive power of H2, we com- pute Kendall’s rank correlation coefficient τ be- tween H2(M) and runtime(M) for each sequence. Kendall’s τ measures how consistently two quanti- ties induce the same ordering over a set of items. A higher value of τ indicates that pairs of items that are ranked higher by one metric are also more likely to be ranked higher by the other, reflecting stronger agreement in their relative ordering. Conversely, values of τ close to zero indicate little systematic agreement beyond chance, while negative values indicate that the two metrics tend to induce oppos- ing orderings. We compute τ separately for each sequence and report aggregate statistics across se- quences, thereby assessing robustness to dataset and hyperparameter variation. Across all 124 sim- ulations, divided into 8 settings we observe a mean Kendall’s τ of −0.964, indicating strong and con- sistent ordering agreement between H2(M) and runtime(M). E.2 Directional Predictive Reliability While rank correlation captures global ordering agreement, it does not directly address the op- erational question of interest: does improving the H2(M) reliably lead to an improvement in runtime(M)? (Pesaran and Timmermann, 1992) To answer this, we analyze parameter intervals updates each sequence. For consecutive values Mi, Mi+1, we compute ∆H2 = H2(Mi+1) −H2(Mi), ∆R = runtime(Mi+1) −runtime(Mi). Conditioning on updates where the Rényi en- tropy decreases (∆(H2) > 0), we measure the conditional success rate Pr (∆R < 0|∆(H2) > 0) , i.e., the probability that runtime decreases despite given an increase in the Rényi entropy. Its comple- ment is defined as a directional success rate. Based on the data collected from 124 simulations, the directional success rate is 92%, implying that in- crease in H2 are followed by runtime reductions in the vast majority of cases. System Prompt You are an intelligent clinical language model. [Discharge Summary Begin] {Discharge Summary} [Discharge Summary End] [Instruction Begin] {Instruction} [Instruction End] [Agent A’s Answer Begin] {A} [Agent A’s Answer End] [Agent B’s Answer Begin] {B} [Agent B’s Answer End] Above, we provide you with a discharge summary and the instruction that the healthcare professional gave about the discharge summary. You are also provided with {Number of Samples} corresponding responses from {Number of Samples} different clinical models. Your task is to read the discharge summary and the instruction carefully, then find the answer to the instruction. Then, compare your answer with each model’s response and evaluate the response based on the following criteria. Criteria:

sections.section_title:
1. Unacceptable (1 point):
The model’s response includes any incorrect or
irrelevant content. If the instruction was unanswerable, the model did not
acknowledge this and outputs the wrong answer.

2. Poor (2 points):
The model’s response does not contain any incorrect or
irrelevant content but omits significant or crucial content required by the
instruction.

3. Satisfactory (3 points): The model’s response does not contain any incorrect
or irrelevant content but omits minor or insignificant required content.

4. Excellent (4 points): The model’s response contains all necessary information.
If the instruction was unanswerable, the model correctly acknowledged this.
sections.text: When evaluating each score, ensure that judgments are not affected by other model responses. The first line must contain only {Number of Samples} values, separated by spaces. Output scores without explanation. Figure 9: Evaluation Judge LLM prompt for Asclepius dataset responses (Figure 2)
